text: This is a patient in his fifties who presented to me with a posterior gist tumor close to the G junction. The gist tumor was a little bit over three centimeter close to the G junction.
I was planning initially on performing a transgastric resection with placing the port into the stomach, but then I changed my mind intraoperatively.
The surgeon opens the gastrocolic ligament and takes down the gastrosplenic ligament, rotating the stomach to locate the posterior gist tumor near the gastroesophageal junction.
The surgeon uses intraoperative endoscopy with TilePro to locate the posterior gist tumor near the gastroesophageal junction.
The surgeon identifies the tumor located very close to the gastroesophageal junction and decides to perform a transgastric resection by making an anterior gastrotomy.

With the assistance of intraoperative endoscopy, the surgeon locates the site for the anterior gastrotomy and uses monopolar scissors to make a transverse incision in the stomach, approximately three to four centimeters in length.
I honestly don't think there is one correct way of doing this procedure. I just felt that this would be the fastest and least painful way of performing the procedure.
After creating the anterior gastrotomy, the surgeon locates the gest tumor on the posterior wall of the stomach. The decision is made to resect the tumor using a stapler for efficiency.

The surgeon uses a blue load stapler to resect the tumor near the G junction, with the endoscope positioned just distal to the junction. The surgeon checks the scope multiple times before firing the stapler to ensure precise placement and avoid narrowing the G junction.
The surgeon uses two loads of a stapler during the procedure, likely to resect the tumor near the G-junction.
The surgeon uses Tile fur to prevent narrowing of the gastroesophageal junction during the procedure. The gist tumor is visually placed into a catch bag for removal.
The surgeon closes the anterior gastronomy in two layers using a NINI V-Lock suture, carefully stitching the incision site.
The surgeon performs a repeat endoscopy to visually inspect the G junction, confirming that it is not narrowed after the procedure.
